title: COVID-19 — Acute Management
oneLiner: SARS-CoV-2 infection causing respiratory tract illness; ranges from mild to critical; immunomodulation and supportive care reduce mortality in severe disease.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)** – look for: respiratory rate >30, SpO₂ <92% on maximal O₂ (non-rebreather), bilateral infiltrates on CXR, rising oxygen requirement → call ICU immediately.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – acute hypoxia out of proportion to lung findings, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, sudden hypotension; single finding: high D-dimer PLUS raised troponin (right heart strain) – in a patient with COVID-19, do not delay CT pulmonary angiogram if clinical suspicion is high.
- **Myocarditis / arrhythmia** – new-onset chest pain, tachyarrhythmias, elevated troponin, ECG changes (diffuse ST elevation or new conduction block); rule out with ECG + troponin + echo if unstable.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever, dry cough, dyspnoea, fatigue, myalgia; anosmia/ageusia (sensitive but not universal); associated: headache, sore throat, diarrhoea, confusion (especially in elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea, tachycardia, low SpO₂, bronchial breath sounds, crackles; may have normal chest auscultation despite hypoxia – silent hypoxaemia is classic.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Influenza** – more rapid onset, high fevers, profound myalgia; test: rapid influenza PCR.
- **Bacterial pneumonia (e.g. Streptococcus pneumoniae)** – high fever, productive cough, focal consolidation on CXR; test: blood cultures, sputum culture.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – if sudden dyspnoea / pleuritic pain, especially with leg swelling; test: D-dimer (if low pre-test probability), CT pulmonary angiogram.
- **Heart failure** – orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, peripheral oedema; test: NT-proBNP, echo.
- **Sepsis of other cause** – if fever + shock + no clear respiratory symptoms; test: blood cultures, lactate.
- **Splitter:** Bilateral ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT with no focal consolidation plus anosmia → COVID-19, not typical bacterial pneumonia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** SpO₂ (check trend), respiratory rate, temperature, NEWS2 score; perform SARS-CoV-2 lateral flow (abbreviated test) but confirm with PCR; ABG if SpO₂ <94% or respiratory distress (check pO₂, pCO₂, lactate).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin, D-dimer, ferritin, coagulation screen, blood cultures (if fever + concern for bacterial co-infection); store serum for future PCR if indicated.
- **Imaging:** CXR (first line) – bilateral, peripheral, hazy opacities; CT thorax (if diagnostic uncertainty or suspicion of PE); lung ultrasound (in experienced hands) – B-lines, consolidation.
- **Special tests:** Serial CRP (tracks severity), troponin (myocardial injury), D-dimer (thrombotic risk), lymphocyte count (lymphopenia typical); repeat PCR may not be needed for clinical management.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** WHO severity criteria – mild (SpO₂ ≥94% room air), moderate (SpO₂ 90-94% with tachypnoea), severe (SpO₂ <90%, respiratory rate >30, ARDS criteria), critical (respiratory failure, shock, multi-organ dysfunction). Also: CURB-65 if bacterial co-infection suspected.
- **Never-miss test:** **SARS-CoV-2 PCR** – without a confirmed diagnosis, you cannot correctly target immunomodulation (dexamethasone, tocilizumab) or inform infection control.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity – if severe (RR >30, SpO₂ <90% on air) → escalate to resus team / ICU review.
  - Oxygen – target SpO₂ ≥94% (if no risk of hypercapnia); for high-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) or non-invasive ventilation (NIV) if refractory hypoxia.
  - If suspected bacterial co-infection → give empirical antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav or doxycycline) only after cultures; do NOT give routinely.
  - Start dexamethasone 6mg PO/IV once daily (if needing supplemental oxygen) – for up to 10 days or until discharge.
  - For patients requiring HFNO or NIV within 24 hours: **tocilizumab** (single IV dose 8mg/kg, max 800mg) OR **baricitinib** (4mg PO once daily for 14 days) – [check local policy] for eligibility (e.g. CRP ≥150 mg/L, require significant oxygen).
  - VTE prophylaxis: choose enoxaparin [dose per local policy based on weight/renal function] – standard prophylaxis unless high bleeding risk.
  - Fluid resuscitation cautiously – avoid overload; use crystalloid (e.g., sodium chloride 0.9%) 500ml bolus only if hypotensive, then reassess.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Daily monitoring: SpO₂, RR, NEWS2, UO, fluid balance; repeat bloods (CRP, U&E, FBC, coagulation) every 24-48h.
  - Maintain dexamethasone course (if indicated).
  - Consider switching from HFNO to NIV or CPAP if worsening – involve respiratory/ICU.
  - If clinical deterioration (rising O₂ requirement, NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory component) → repeat CXR, check for bacterial superinfection (sputum culture, CRP trend), consider tocilizumab/baricitinib if not already given and still within window.
  - Thromboprophylaxis: continue until discharge or 7 days (whichever is longer); if VTE suspected → urgent CTPA.
  - Nutrition – encourage oral intake; if severe illness, consider NG feeding early.

- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most patients recover fully; arrange GP follow-up for persistent symptoms.
  - For those with severe illness (ICU stay) → refer to post-COVID clinic or paediatric equivalent for pulmonary rehab, psychological support.
  - Anti-SARS-CoV-2 treatment: neutralising monoclonal antibodies (e.g., casirivimab/imdevimab) reserved for high-risk outpatients – [check local policy] (rarely used now as dominant variant not susceptible).

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV sodium chloride 0.9% 1ml/kg/h maintenance (but keep euvolemic – avoid overload).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (adjust if weight <50kg or renal impairment; alternative: tinzaparin or dalteparin per local policy).
  - Diet: light/soft diet if able to eat; NBM only if intubated or severe dysphagia.
  - Observations: NEWS2 at least 4-hourly (or more frequent if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Dexamethasone 6mg PO/IV once daily for up to 10 days (start if requiring supplemental oxygen).
  - Tocilizumab (RoActemra) 8mg/kg IV single dose (max 800mg) – within 24h of requiring high-flow oxygen or NIV.
  - Baricitinib (Olumiant) 4mg PO once daily for 14 days – if tocilizumab not available or contraindicated.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Remdesivir (Veklury) – for hospitalised patients with SpO₂ <94% but not requiring invasive ventilation; dose: 200mg IV on day 1, then 100mg IV once daily for 5-10 days for patients on supplemental oxygen but not on high-flow or mechanical ventilation (per NICE)
  - Corticosteroid: prednisolone 40mg PO once daily if dexamethasone not available (equivalent).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tocilizumab: caution in active TB, severe active infection (if already on broad-spectrum antibiotics may still be okay), neutrophils <1.0, platelets <50, or transaminases >5x ULN.
  - Baricitinib: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min; contraindicated in pregnancy; do not start if absolute neutrophil count <1.0.
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines; monitor blood glucose; taper if course >10 days (though typical course is short).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Dexamethasone: no adjustment.
  - Tocilizumab: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal/hepatic impairment; avoid if ALT >5x ULN.
  - Baricitinib: reduce to 2mg OD if eGFR 30-60; avoid if eGFR <30.
  - Remdesivir: not recommended if eGFR <30 unless strong indication (check local policy).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Dexamethasone prescribed for patients NOT requiring oxygen – this is NOT indicated and may cause harm (hyperglycaemia, immunosuppression). Tocilizumab given too early (before significant hypoxia) – reserve for severe disease only.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** ARDS, VTE (PE/DVT), myocarditis, arrhythmias, secondary bacterial pneumonia, acute kidney injury (AKI), hyperglycaemia, deranged LFTs, disseminated intravascular coagulation.
- **Chronic:** Post-COVID syndrome (fatigue, dyspnoea, cognitive impairment, persistent anosmia/ageusia), pulmonary fibrosis (rare), deconditioning, psychological trauma.
sections.Prognosis: - Mild disease: full recovery in 2-4 weeks. Severe disease (requiring ICU): mortality 20-30%, significant risk of long-term pulmonary and functional impairment. Older age, obesity, diabetes, malignancy, male sex associated with worse outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do:** Start dexamethasone as soon as you confirm they need oxygen – earlier is better. Check blood glucose daily – hyperglycaemia is common.
- **Do:** Use a formal risk assessment for VTE prophylaxis; for obese patients use higher prophylactic dose (e.g., enoxaparin 0.5mg/kg BD per local policy).
- **Do:** Encourage awake proning in ward patients with moderate hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on Fio₂ >0.4) – improves oxygenation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t:** Give dexamethasone to a patient with mild disease (SpO₂ ≥94% on room air) – no benefit, possible harm.
- **Don’t:** Ignore rising D-dimer – it may indicate VTE; do not treat with heparin if no clinical suspicion – investigate first with CTPA if appropriate.
- **Don’t:** Over-empirically give antibiotics – bacterial co-infection is rare (<10%); use only if clinical or radiological evidence of bacterial infection.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness (e.g., unable to speak full sentences), SpO₂ <92% on room air (if patient owns a pulse oximeter), new chest pain or haemoptysis, confusion or drowsiness, inability to keep fluids down.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48-72 hours; if severe disease or complications (e.g., PE, myocarditis) – specialist clinic (respiratory/cardiology) at 6 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory component (RR >25, SpO₂ <92%), lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, new arrhythmia, hypotension refractory to fluids, requirement for non-rebreather (FiO₂ >0.6) with SpO₂ unstable (<92%).
- **Specific thresholds:** SpO₂ <90% despite maximal support (e.g., HFNO at 60 L/min + FiO₂ 0.6) → discuss for intubation. Lactate rising >3 in the setting of sepsis → sepsis 6 pathway.

rawMarkdown:
## COVID-19 — Acute Management
**One-liner:** SARS-CoV-2 infection causing respiratory tract illness; ranges from mild to critical; immunomodulation and supportive care reduce mortality in severe disease.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)** – look for: respiratory rate >30, SpO₂ <92% on maximal O₂ (non-rebreather), bilateral infiltrates on CXR, rising oxygen requirement → call ICU immediately.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – acute hypoxia out of proportion to lung findings, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, sudden hypotension; single finding: high D-dimer PLUS raised troponin (right heart strain) – in a patient with COVID-19, do not delay CT pulmonary angiogram if clinical suspicion is high.
- **Myocarditis / arrhythmia** – new-onset chest pain, tachyarrhythmias, elevated troponin, ECG changes (diffuse ST elevation or new conduction block); rule out with ECG + troponin + echo if unstable.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever, dry cough, dyspnoea, fatigue, myalgia; anosmia/ageusia (sensitive but not universal); associated: headache, sore throat, diarrhoea, confusion (especially in elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** tachypnoea, tachycardia, low SpO₂, bronchial breath sounds, crackles; may have normal chest auscultation despite hypoxia – silent hypoxaemia is classic.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Influenza** – more rapid onset, high fevers, profound myalgia; test: rapid influenza PCR.
- **Bacterial pneumonia (e.g. Streptococcus pneumoniae)** – high fever, productive cough, focal consolidation on CXR; test: blood cultures, sputum culture.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – if sudden dyspnoea / pleuritic pain, especially with leg swelling; test: D-dimer (if low pre-test probability), CT pulmonary angiogram.
- **Heart failure** – orthopnoea, PND, raised JVP, peripheral oedema; test: NT-proBNP, echo.
- **Sepsis of other cause** – if fever + shock + no clear respiratory symptoms; test: blood cultures, lactate.
- **Splitter:** Bilateral ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT with no focal consolidation plus anosmia → COVID-19, not typical bacterial pneumonia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** SpO₂ (check trend), respiratory rate, temperature, NEWS2 score; perform SARS-CoV-2 lateral flow (abbreviated test) but confirm with PCR; ABG if SpO₂ <94% or respiratory distress (check pO₂, pCO₂, lactate).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin, D-dimer, ferritin, coagulation screen, blood cultures (if fever + concern for bacterial co-infection); store serum for future PCR if indicated.
- **Imaging:** CXR (first line) – bilateral, peripheral, hazy opacities; CT thorax (if diagnostic uncertainty or suspicion of PE); lung ultrasound (in experienced hands) – B-lines, consolidation.
- **Special tests:** Serial CRP (tracks severity), troponin (myocardial injury), D-dimer (thrombotic risk), lymphocyte count (lymphopenia typical); repeat PCR may not be needed for clinical management.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** WHO severity criteria – mild (SpO₂ ≥94% room air), moderate (SpO₂ 90-94% with tachypnoea), severe (SpO₂ <90%, respiratory rate >30, ARDS criteria), critical (respiratory failure, shock, multi-organ dysfunction). Also: CURB-65 if bacterial co-infection suspected.
- **Never-miss test:** **SARS-CoV-2 PCR** – without a confirmed diagnosis, you cannot correctly target immunomodulation (dexamethasone, tocilizumab) or inform infection control.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity – if severe (RR >30, SpO₂ <90% on air) → escalate to resus team / ICU review.
  - Oxygen – target SpO₂ ≥94% (if no risk of hypercapnia); for high-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) or non-invasive ventilation (NIV) if refractory hypoxia.
  - If suspected bacterial co-infection → give empirical antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav or doxycycline) only after cultures; do NOT give routinely.
  - Start dexamethasone 6mg PO/IV once daily (if needing supplemental oxygen) – for up to 10 days or until discharge.
  - For patients requiring HFNO or NIV within 24 hours: **tocilizumab** (single IV dose 8mg/kg, max 800mg) OR **baricitinib** (4mg PO once daily for 14 days) – [check local policy] for eligibility (e.g. CRP ≥150 mg/L, require significant oxygen).
  - VTE prophylaxis: choose enoxaparin [dose per local policy based on weight/renal function] – standard prophylaxis unless high bleeding risk.
  - Fluid resuscitation cautiously – avoid overload; use crystalloid (e.g., sodium chloride 0.9%) 500ml bolus only if hypotensive, then reassess.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Daily monitoring: SpO₂, RR, NEWS2, UO, fluid balance; repeat bloods (CRP, U&E, FBC, coagulation) every 24-48h.
  - Maintain dexamethasone course (if indicated).
  - Consider switching from HFNO to NIV or CPAP if worsening – involve respiratory/ICU.
  - If clinical deterioration (rising O₂ requirement, NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory component) → repeat CXR, check for bacterial superinfection (sputum culture, CRP trend), consider tocilizumab/baricitinib if not already given and still within window.
  - Thromboprophylaxis: continue until discharge or 7 days (whichever is longer); if VTE suspected → urgent CTPA.
  - Nutrition – encourage oral intake; if severe illness, consider NG feeding early.

- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most patients recover fully; arrange GP follow-up for persistent symptoms.
  - For those with severe illness (ICU stay) → refer to post-COVID clinic or paediatric equivalent for pulmonary rehab, psychological support.
  - Anti-SARS-CoV-2 treatment: neutralising monoclonal antibodies (e.g., casirivimab/imdevimab) reserved for high-risk outpatients – [check local policy] (rarely used now as dominant variant not susceptible).

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV sodium chloride 0.9% 1ml/kg/h maintenance (but keep euvolemic – avoid overload).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (adjust if weight <50kg or renal impairment; alternative: tinzaparin or dalteparin per local policy).
  - Diet: light/soft diet if able to eat; NBM only if intubated or severe dysphagia.
  - Observations: NEWS2 at least 4-hourly (or more frequent if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Dexamethasone 6mg PO/IV once daily for up to 10 days (start if requiring supplemental oxygen).
  - Tocilizumab (RoActemra) 8mg/kg IV single dose (max 800mg) – within 24h of requiring high-flow oxygen or NIV.
  - Baricitinib (Olumiant) 4mg PO once daily for 14 days – if tocilizumab not available or contraindicated.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Remdesivir (Veklury) – for hospitalised patients with SpO₂ <94% but not requiring invasive ventilation; dose: 200mg IV on day 1, then 100mg IV once daily for 5-10 days for patients on supplemental oxygen but not on high-flow or mechanical ventilation (per NICE)
  - Corticosteroid: prednisolone 40mg PO once daily if dexamethasone not available (equivalent).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tocilizumab: caution in active TB, severe active infection (if already on broad-spectrum antibiotics may still be okay), neutrophils <1.0, platelets <50, or transaminases >5x ULN.
  - Baricitinib: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min; contraindicated in pregnancy; do not start if absolute neutrophil count <1.0.
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines; monitor blood glucose; taper if course >10 days (though typical course is short).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Dexamethasone: no adjustment.
  - Tocilizumab: no dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal/hepatic impairment; avoid if ALT >5x ULN.
  - Baricitinib: reduce to 2mg OD if eGFR 30-60; avoid if eGFR <30.
  - Remdesivir: not recommended if eGFR <30 unless strong indication (check local policy).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Dexamethasone prescribed for patients NOT requiring oxygen – this is NOT indicated and may cause harm (hyperglycaemia, immunosuppression). Tocilizumab given too early (before significant hypoxia) – reserve for severe disease only.

### Complications
- **Acute:** ARDS, VTE (PE/DVT), myocarditis, arrhythmias, secondary bacterial pneumonia, acute kidney injury (AKI), hyperglycaemia, deranged LFTs, disseminated intravascular coagulation.
- **Chronic:** Post-COVID syndrome (fatigue, dyspnoea, cognitive impairment, persistent anosmia/ageusia), pulmonary fibrosis (rare), deconditioning, psychological trauma.

### Prognosis
- Mild disease: full recovery in 2-4 weeks. Severe disease (requiring ICU): mortality 20-30%, significant risk of long-term pulmonary and functional impairment. Older age, obesity, diabetes, malignancy, male sex associated with worse outcomes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do:** Start dexamethasone as soon as you confirm they need oxygen – earlier is better. Check blood glucose daily – hyperglycaemia is common.
- **Do:** Use a formal risk assessment for VTE prophylaxis; for obese patients use higher prophylactic dose (e.g., enoxaparin 0.5mg/kg BD per local policy).
- **Do:** Encourage awake proning in ward patients with moderate hypoxia (SpO₂ <94% on Fio₂ >0.4) – improves oxygenation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t:** Give dexamethasone to a patient with mild disease (SpO₂ ≥94% on room air) – no benefit, possible harm.
- **Don’t:** Ignore rising D-dimer – it may indicate VTE; do not treat with heparin if no clinical suspicion – investigate first with CTPA if appropriate.
- **Don’t:** Over-empirically give antibiotics – bacterial co-infection is rare (<10%); use only if clinical or radiological evidence of bacterial infection.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness (e.g., unable to speak full sentences), SpO₂ <92% on room air (if patient owns a pulse oximeter), new chest pain or haemoptysis, confusion or drowsiness, inability to keep fluids down.
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48-72 hours; if severe disease or complications (e.g., PE, myocarditis) – specialist clinic (respiratory/cardiology) at 6 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory component (RR >25, SpO₂ <92%), lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, new arrhythmia, hypotension refractory to fluids, requirement for non-rebreather (FiO₂ >0.6) with SpO₂ unstable (<92%).
- **Specific thresholds:** SpO₂ <90% despite maximal support (e.g., HFNO at 60 L/min + FiO₂ 0.6) → discuss for intubation. Lactate rising >3 in the setting of sepsis → sepsis 6 pathway.
